3A_Bibliographic.citation: Nagata, J. M., Sajjad, O. M., Smith, N., Zamora, G., Dhama, S., Al-shoaibi, A. A., Ganson, K. T., Testa, A., Moreno, M. A., Kiss, O., Baker, F. C., & Jackson, D. B. (2024). Social media use and alcohol sipping in early adolescents: A prospective cohort study. Substance Use & Misuse, 59(6), 971-976.

3B_Sample_Data.verbatim_sample_description: We analyzed prospective data from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study (Years 1-3; 5.0 release), a large diverse nationwide sample of 9-10-year-olds at baseline (2016-2018) from 21 research sites across the U.S. ... Participants with missing data for social media use and alcohol sipping were excluded (n=4361; Appendix A). ... Of the 7514 participants who met our inclusion criteria, 49.8% were female and 42.8% were racial/ethnic minorities.

3C_Exposure.exposure_variable: Time spent on social media (hours per day), calculated as weighted average of weekday and weekend use
3C_Exposure.exposure_instrument: ABCD Youth Screen Time Survey - self-reported hours of typical time spent on social media
3C_Exposure.operationalization: Continuous hours/day; weighted sum ([weekday average x 5] + [weekend average x 2])/7
3C_Exposure.verbatim_exposure_description: Time spent on social media was collected using children's self-reported hours of typical time (hours and minutes) spent on social media, separately for weekdays and weekend days. Total daily social media time was calculated as the weighted sum ([weekday average x 5] + [weekend average x 2])/7.

3D_Outcome.outcome_instrument: Timeline Follow-Back (TLFB) instrument - adapted version asking whether children had tried a sip of alcohol
3D_Outcome.verbatim_outcome_description: Participants in the ABCD study completed an adapted version of the Timeline Follow-Back (TLFB) instrument, which is a retrospective, calendar-based self-report instrument for measuring the use of alcohol and other addictive substances. The current analysis used the question on the TLFB asking whether children had tried a sip of alcohol such as beer, wine, or liquor (rum, vodka, gin, and whiskey) since the last visit, which was assessed at Years 1 through 3.

3E_Design_Analysis.model_type_detail: Modified Poisson regression (risk ratios transformed from Poisson) with GSEM for mediation; propensity weights applied
3E_Design_Analysis.covariates_listed: age, sex, race/ethnicity, household income, parent education, child religiosity, depression symptoms (CBCL), anxiety symptoms (CBCL), study site, alcohol sipping at Year 1
3E_Design_Analysis.verbatim_analysis_description: Multiple modified Poisson regression analyses transformed into risk ratios were conducted, adjusting for covariates listed above and controlling for alcohol sipping at Year 1 in three models including: Model 1: social media time (independent variable) on sipping alcohol (dependent variable); Model 2: problematic social media use (mediator variable) on sipping alcohol (dependent variable); and Model 3: social media time (independent variable) and problematic social media use on sipping alcohol (dependent variable). We used generalized structural equation modeling (GSEM) to assess the mediating effect of problematic social media use. Two-sided alpha was set at p<0.05.

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Risk ratio per hour/day of social media
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Social media time (Year 1) was prospectively associated with 1.31 (95% confidence interval 1.20-1.43) times higher risk of new-onset sipping (Year 3)

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Risk ratio per unit increase in SMAQ score
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Problematic social media use (Year 2) was associated with sipping alcohol (Year 3, Model 2): 1.03 (1.03, 1.05), p<0.001

3F_Effect_Sizes.predictor: Social media time (Year 1, hours/day) - adjusted for mediator
3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Risk ratio per hour/day, adjusted for problematic social media use
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Model 3: Social media time Risk Ratio 1.21 (1.10, 1.33), p<0.001

3F_Effect_Sizes.predictor: Problematic social media use score (Year 2) - in Model 3
3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Risk ratio per unit SMAQ score, adjusted for SM time
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Model 3: Problematic social media use 1.03 (1.02, 1.04), p<0.001

3F_Effect_Sizes.predictor: Social media time - indirect effect through problematic SM use
3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Indirect effect; bias-corrected CIs from 5000 bootstrap samples. 25.0% of association mediated.
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Table 3: Indirect effect through problematic social media use score: B (SE) = 0.011 (0.0021), BC 95% CI (0.007, 0.016), p<0.001. Proportion mediated: 25.0 (95% CI 11.0, 39.3), p=0.001

3F_Effect_Sizes.predictor: Parent education: college or more (vs HS or less)
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Table 2 Model 1: College education or more 0.67 (0.49, 0.91), p=0.011

3G_Reporting_Quality.notes: The primary RR of 1.31 per hour/day of social media is moderate for a risk ratio. The paper does not discuss the magnitude of this effect relative to clinical meaningfulness. Bold formatting in Table 2 indicates p<0.05.

3H_Recommendations.recommendation_verbatim: Pediatricians can encourage parents to develop a family media use plan which could involve regular discussions with their children about online activities, privacy settings, limits on social media use, and prevention of problematic use. Policies that protect early adolescents on social media from exposure to alcohol-related content, such as more robust age verification and enhancement of parental controls and privacy settings, should be implemented.

3I_Rhetorical_Moves.per_unit_verbatim: Social media time (Year 1) was prospectively associated with 1.31 (95% confidence interval 1.20-1.43) times higher risk of new-onset sipping (Year 3)
3I_Rhetorical_Moves.discussion_characterization: The discussion frames social media use as a meaningful risk factor for early adolescent alcohol sipping, emphasizing the prospective design as a strength over prior cross-sectional work. It highlights the mediation pathway through problematic social media use (25% mediated) and draws on the Media Practice Model to explain mechanisms. Policy recommendations for age verification and parental controls are made. The magnitude of the primary effect (RR=1.31 per hour/day) is not explicitly contextualized as small or large.
